text.title: Acceptance Criteria Rules and Critical Path
text.summary: Rules for writing testable AC items, good vs bad AC examples, critical path structure, and Technical Notes guidelines for task files.

rule.statement: Every AC item must be an observable outcome with a specific, measurable value. HTTP status codes, database state assertions, or measurable values are acceptable. Vague phrases are not.
rule.rationale: Vague ACs cannot be mechanically verified by the executor. "Works correctly" leaves the success determination to the agent, which may mark a failing task as done.

example.code: **Acceptance Criteria:** - [ ] POST /api/auth/register returns 201 on success - [ ] Response includes valid JWT token (verified with jwt.verify) - [ ] Password is bcrypt hashed in database (SELECT query confirms) - [ ] Duplicate email returns 409 with error message - [ ] Invalid email format returns 400 with validation errors
example.note: Each item is specific, references an observable outcome, and has a clear pass/fail condition.

example.code: **Acceptance Criteria:** - [ ] Registration works correctly - [ ] Handles errors properly - [ ] Follows best practices - [ ] Code is clean
example.note: Vague, subjective, not measurable. No clear test procedure. Multiple interpretations possible.

rule.statement: Write Tests checklist separately from functional ACs. Use the Tests section for unit/integration/E2E items so test coverage is explicit and distinct from behavioral requirements.
rule.rationale: Mixing test requirements with behavioral ACs obscures which ACs require code changes and which require test additions. The executor can address them separately.

rule.statement: Mark the critical path (longest dependency chain) explicitly in the implementation plan. The executor will optimize for parallelism but may miss the longest chain without explicit marking.
rule.rationale: Unidentified critical path causes misallocation of wave priorities. The longest chain determines the minimum elapsed sessions for the feature; shortening it has more impact than any non-critical task optimization.

example.code: Critical Path: TASK-001 (15k) -> TASK-003 (45k) -> TASK-005 (30k) -> TASK-007 (75k) Total: ~165k tokens across 4 serial tasks Parallel path: TASK-002 -> TASK-004 -> TASK-006 (joins at TASK-007)

rule.statement: Technical Notes must cite the exact file path and pattern to follow, not generic advice. Write them after Wave 1 is complete for later-wave tasks, not speculatively.
rule.rationale: Speculative Technical Notes written before Wave 1 execution become stale when implementation patterns differ from design assumptions. They mislead the executor.

list.item: Good: "Use Joi for validation (see constitution.md section Standards)"
Good: "Follow error handling pattern in src/handlers/user.ts lines 45-67"
Bad: "Make sure to handle errors properly"

antipattern.description: Agents generating task files write "Depends on: None" even when implicit data dependencies exist (e.g., handler task that needs a database migration).
antipattern.reason: Implicit dependencies not declared cause wave ordering to place the handler before the migration. Validate that database migration tasks always precede handler tasks.
